one_line_takeaway: SimMIA shows that a black-box attacker with only text-generation access to an LLM -- no logits, no tokenizer -- can perform training-data membership inference nearly as well as attackers with internal model access, by sampling next words one at a time and scoring them with soft semantic similarity rather than exact N-gram matches.
key_finding: SimMIA substantially closes the gap between black-box and gray-box MIA: it improves average AUC over the best black-box baseline (SaMIA) by 16.6 points on WikiMIA and 14.9 points on MIMIR, and on WikiMIA-25 (modern proprietary LLMs) it beats SaMIA by +21.7 AUC and +25.8 TPR@5%FPR on average. It trails the best gray-box method by only 6.1 (WikiMIA) and 3.4 (MIMIR) AUC points on average -- a gap comparable to the spread between the top two gray-box methods themselves (10.2 and 0.7 AUC) -- and on some settings (e.g., OPT-6.7B on WikiMIA) it surpasses the best gray-box baseline outright. Ablations confirm both novel components matter: removing word-by-word sampling drops AUC on WikiMIA from 70.1/75.4/73.2 (lengths 32/64/128) to 55.8/50.1/47.7, and removing relative aggregation drops it to 57.4/53.7/57.0. The hard-indicator variant SimMIA* actually outperforms the soft-scoring SimMIA on WikiMIA-style benchmarks but generalizes worse to MIMIR (SimMIA* beats SaMIA by only +7.6 AUC on MIMIR vs. SimMIA's +14.9), motivating the soft scoring choice as the more robust default. When richer API metadata is available, SimMIA can exploit it: adding tokenization visibility raises GPT-5-Chat AUC from 82.0 to 83.1 and GPT-4.1-mini from 85.3 to 87.0, and adding token log-probabilities raises GPT-4.1-mini further to 93.7 AUC.
core_contribution: The paper proposes SimMIA, a strictly black-box membership inference attack (MIA) against LLMs that requires access only to sampled generated text (no logits, no tokenizer). SimMIA replaces prior sampling-based MIA's full-continuation sampling with word-by-word next-word sampling from a fixed prefix (avoiding self-conditioning/distribution drift from multi-step generation), and replaces exact N-gram/ROUGE matching with a soft semantic scoring function that measures cosine similarity (via word embeddings) between the ground-truth next word and sampled candidate words, aggregated relative to a score computed with a prepended block of known non-member text ('relative aggregation', following Xie et al. 2024's ReCaLL idea but applied to the black-box regime). The paper also contributes WikiMIA-25, a new benchmark built from 2015-2016 (member) vs. March-September 2025 (non-member) Wikipedia events, specifically constructed to remain valid against recently released proprietary models (Qwen3-8B-Base, Claude-4.5-Haiku, Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-5-Chat) whose training cutoffs make older benchmarks like WikiMIA-24 stale.
threat_model: Attacker (auditor) has strictly black-box API access to a target LLM: no model weights, no logits/per-token probabilities, and no visibility into the tokenizer -- only the ability to submit a prefix and observe generated text continuations. This is explicitly contrasted in the paper with a 'gray-box' setting (used by most prior MIA baselines) where the attacker can see per-token logits and/or tokenization. Goal of the attacker is to determine, given a candidate text x, whether x was part of the target model's training data D -- i.e., an auditing capability for copyright infringement, training-data privacy leakage, and benchmark/data contamination detection, not an attack that manipulates or compromises the model.
limitations: The paper's own stated Limitations section: SimMIA shares the fundamental limitation of all sampling-based black-box MIAs -- it requires repeated API querying (rather than a single forward pass as in gray-box methods) to approximate model behavior, incurring higher computational overhead, latency, and monetary cost; the paper flags improving sample efficiency of this whole class of methods as future work. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: performance is uneven across target models -- on LLaMA-13B/13B-family models SimMIA's AUC (e.g., ~59-67 across lengths) is markedly weaker than on OPT-6.7B or GPT-NeoX-20B, and on WikiMIA-25 SimMIA trails the gray-box ReCaLL baseline by a wide margin on Pythia-6.9B (85.0 vs 96.1 AUC) and Qwen3-8B-Base (74.9 vs 91.0 AUC); the method is sensitive to the composition of non-member prefixes (random-prefix AUC standard deviation ~2.90 vs ~0.86 for a fixed prefix set) and to prefix-domain match (out-of-domain non-member prefixes degrade AUC substantially, e.g., 70.1->51.3 on Wikipedia-domain length-32 when non-members are drawn from mismatched domains); and WikiMIA-25's proprietary-model evaluation is restricted to a small balanced subset (120 members/120 non-members) for API cost reasons, and depends on models correctly following an instruction-only single-token-completion prompt rather than doing natural continuation, which is itself an approximation of black-box next-token access.

datasets_used: WikiMIA-25 (this paper's new benchmark: Jan 2015-Dec 2016 Wikipedia events = members, March-Sept 2025 events = non-members; 759/875/529 member-nonmember pairs for lengths 32/64/128; 120 members + 120 non-members subsampled from length-32 split for proprietary-model experiments)
MIMIR (Duan et al. 2024), 7-gram version, splits: Wikipedia, Github, Pile CC, PubMed Central, ArXiv, DM Mathematics, HackerNews
quantitative_results: WikiMIA average AUC: SimMIA improves over SaMIA (black-box SOTA) by 16.6 AUC points; trails best gray-box method by 6.1 AUC points on average
MIMIR average AUC: SimMIA improves over SaMIA by 14.9 AUC points; trails best gray-box method by 3.4 AUC points
WikiMIA-25: SimMIA beats SaMIA by +21.7 AUC and +25.8 TPR@5%FPR on average across Pythia-6.9B, Qwen3-8B-Base, Claude-4.5-Haiku, Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-5-Chat
Ablation on WikiMIA (AUC, lengths 32/64/128): full SimMIA 70.1/75.4/73.2; w/o word-by-word sampling 55.8/50.1/47.7; w/o relative aggregation 57.4/53.7/57.0
SimMIA* (hard-indicator scoring) vs SaMIA: +19.2 AUC on WikiMIA-style datasets but only +7.6 AUC on MIMIR, vs SimMIA (soft scoring) +15.4 AUC on WikiMIA-style and +14.9 AUC on MIMIR
Progressive disclosure on WikiMIA-25: SimMIA text-only AUC 82.0 (GPT-5-Chat) / 85.3 (GPT-4.1-mini); +tokenization 83.1 / 87.0; +token log-probs (GPT-4.1-mini only) 93.7
Prefix stability (WikiMIA, 5 runs): fixed non-member prefix set stdev ~0.86 vs random-prefix stdev ~2.90; random-prefix mean 72.77 still beats SaMIA's 60.38
Cross-domain prefix degradation (length 32): in-domain (Original) 70.1 AUC vs out-of-domain non-member prefixes: Wikipedia 51.3, arXiv 57.6, GitHub 56.0
baselines_compared: Gray-box: Loss, Reference (Carlini et al. 2022), Lowercase, Zlib (Carlini et al. 2021), Neighbor (Mattern et al. 2023), Min-K% (Shi et al. 2024), Min-K%++ (Zhang et al. 2024a), ReCaLL (Xie et al. 2024), PETAL (He et al. 2025)
Black-box: SaMIA (Kaneko et al. 2025) -- the paper's primary black-box comparator, and SimMIA* (this paper's own hard-indicator ablation variant)
